explicit_text: My name is Michael Luke James, but I usually go by Luke because my dad is also named Michael. I am an associate professor in anesthesiology and neurology at Duke University, primarily working in the neuro intensive care unit. During the COVID-19 pandemic, my clinical responsibilities shifted somewhat, especially with the increased number of patients requiring isolation. I experienced a lot of stress due to the unknowns of the disease and the challenges of communicating with families during visitation restrictions.
implicit_text: My name is Michael Luke James, but I usually go by Luke because my dad is also named Michael. I am an associate professor in anesthesiology and neurology at a major university in North Carolina, primarily working in the neuro intensive care unit. During the early 2020 global health crisis, my clinical responsibilities shifted somewhat, especially with the increased number of patients requiring isolation. I experienced a lot of stress due to the unknowns of the disease and the challenges of communicating with families during visitation restrictions.

explicit_text: I practice mostly in the neuro intensive care unit at Duke University Hospital, where we care for patients with neurological injuries like stroke and brain trauma. During the COVID-19 pandemic, the hospital saw a dramatic shift in patient burden, especially with many patients requiring isolation in negative pressure rooms. Early on, the hospital implemented strict visitation policies, which meant families could not visit their loved ones, creating significant emotional challenges. Over time, we adapted by using technology to facilitate communication between patients and families.
implicit_text: I practice mostly in the neuro intensive care unit at a large academic medical center in Durham, North Carolina, where we care for patients with neurological injuries like stroke and brain trauma. During the early 2020 global health crisis, the facility saw a dramatic shift in patient burden, especially with many patients requiring isolation in negative pressure rooms. Early on, strict visitation policies were implemented, which meant families could not visit their loved ones, creating significant emotional challenges. Over time, we adapted by using technology to facilitate communication between patients and families.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic drastically changed my work and the hospital environment starting in early 2020. Initially, there was a lot of uncertainty and stress due to unknowns about the disease, lack of testing, and rapidly evolving institutional policies. We saw many patients requiring strict isolation, which complicated care and severely limited family visitation. The emotional toll of patients dying alone and families being unable to visit was profound, and we had to rely on technology to bridge that gap as best we could.
implicit_text: The global respiratory disease outbreak that began in early 2020 drastically changed my work and the hospital environment. Initially, there was a lot of uncertainty and stress due to unknowns about the illness, lack of testing, and rapidly evolving institutional policies. We saw many patients requiring strict isolation, which complicated care and severely limited family visitation. The emotional toll of patients dying alone and families being unable to visit was profound, and we had to rely on technology to bridge that gap as best we could.

explicit_text: Wuhan was where the COVID-19 outbreak was first experienced months before it hit the US, and although we had some knowledge from their experience, many unknowns remained when the pandemic reached us. In March and April 2020, we were still learning about the disease and how to manage it effectively. The early experience in Wuhan informed some of our institutional responses, but the stress and uncertainty were still very high here at Duke University Hospital.
implicit_text: The city in central China was where the initial outbreak of the novel coronavirus was first experienced months before it hit the US, and although we had some knowledge from their experience, many unknowns remained when the crisis reached us. In March and April 2020, we were still learning about the disease and how to manage it effectively. The early experience in that city informed some of our institutional responses, but the stress and uncertainty were still very high here at the medical center in North Carolina.

explicit_text: While I did not work in Italy, the situation there was part of the global context as COVID-19 spread. Italy experienced a severe outbreak months before many parts of the US, which contributed to our understanding of the disease's impact. Observing Italy's challenges helped inform our preparations and responses at Duke University Hospital, especially regarding patient isolation and resource management during the pandemic.
implicit_text: While I did not work in the European country that faced a severe outbreak early in 2020, the situation there was part of the global context as the respiratory illness spread. That country experienced a severe outbreak months before many parts of the US, which contributed to our understanding of the disease's impact. Observing their challenges helped inform our preparations and responses at the medical center in Durham, especially regarding patient isolation and resource management during the crisis.
